title: What Wisdom Do I Have Now That I Didn't at 30?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for naming what getting older has actually given you
excerpt: A journal prompt set for the woman who knows she has learned things from getting older but has never sat down to name them. Moves from what she knows now to the cost at which she learned it and the freedom it has bought.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on wisdom-and-ageing literature from Erik Erikson's life-stage model and contemporary positive-ageing writing.

practice.whenToUse: Use around a birthday, or any time you notice yourself thinking of age only as loss.

content.text: A journal prompt set for the second half of life, or any time in a life when you stop and notice that you know things now that you didn't at 30. Naming the wisdom is the practice.
What does getting older feel like to me right now?
Write the real answer. Both the good and the difficult.
What would 30-year-old me not believe about the life I have now?
What she would be surprised by. What she would not have expected.
Name three things you know now that you did not know at 30.
About people, about yourself, about what matters. Concrete things, not generalities.
What did getting here cost you?
The wisdom was not free. Write what you paid for it.
What are you freer to do, or not do, now that you are older?
Name the freedoms. Some of them are quiet. Write them anyway.

Written for homemade.education, drawing on Erik Erikson's model of adult development (particularly his 'generativity vs stagnation' and 'integrity vs despair' stages) and contemporary positive-ageing writing.
